item.title: Lessons from Brexit for the world economy
item.description: Ten years on, it’s clear the warnings should have been heeded

item.title: They think it’s oil over
item.description: Plus gilts on the comeback trail

item.title: Patent cliffs aren’t the only driver of biotech M&A boom
item.description: Many deals this year have been for early-stage assets

item.title: Boaz Weinstein is half right on Workspace’s valuation rut
item.description: London’s commercial property market is lumpy and slowing

item.title: The future of TV: lots of choice, lots of hassle
item.description: The fracturing of platforms has created both delight and frustration for viewers

item.title: What if ideas aren’t getting harder to find, after all?
item.description: Large companies may have just become better at hiding the fruits of their labour

item.title: What are the legal considerations for a destination wedding?
item.description: We want to know about the potential pitfalls of marrying abroad

item.title: The profits boom is a mixed blessing
item.description: The Kalecki-Levy equation suggests some awkward trade-offs for the US government and markets

item.title: What Ambani’s deep-tech ambitions reveal about India’s AI limits
item.description: Country still needs to play catch-up if it is to become self-reliant in the use of the technology

item.title: UK takeover targets are right to set the bar high
item.description: Rejecting bids is not a riskless strategy. But this time boards may have a point

item.title: Latin America’s shallow shift to the right
item.description: Colombia and other recent polls highlight polarisation rather than a decisive swing

item.title: Credible deterrence requires a new Nato bank
item.description: Capital rules prevent the defence ecosystem from getting financing at the volume it needs from commercial lenders

item.title: The emigrants Israel can’t afford to lose
item.description: Those inclined to leave are the ones sustaining what’s left of the country’s liberal democracy

item.title: Why is getting to a World Cup game so hard?
item.description: Fans travelling to the stadium in New Jersey are having to contend with high train fares, limited bus seats and a ban on walking or cycling

item.title: Putin’s system is in a state of slow implosion
item.description: Look to the erosion of fiscal discipline to see where the problems lie

item.title: Burnham’s first hire helps win over his doubters
item.description: James Purnell, set to be chief of staff, reassures Labour MPs about presumptive PM’s management of the economy

item.title: SpaceX stumbles
item.description: Ordinary IPO behaviour

item.title: Heatwave shows we are marinating in a crisis of our own making
item.description: Political inaction in the battle against climate change is costly and getting costlier

item.title: Struggling European carmakers have options, none of them good
item.description: Forging alliances with China would help the struggling sector offload some of its bloated costs. It may be the pick of a bad bunch

item.title: Parents have shouldered VAT on school fees with a groan
item.description: The decline in privately educated pupils has not been as big as many — including me — anticipated

item.title: The rise and fall of US hegemony
item.description: On the eve of its 250th birthday, America and the world order it created are in crisis

item.title: Gilt investors shouldn’t think so much about British politics
item.description: The risks of decisions in Westminster are overplayed compared with global turmoil

item.title: Experts know less than they like to think
item.description: In a world of untested treatments, disputed policies and untried inventions, it can’t be hard to remember why evidence is so important

item.title: Private equity’s move into youth sports brings some truths home
item.description: Investments create benefits but also lead to higher costs and risk of inequality

item.title: The US Treasury market rests on shakier ground
item.description: Reforms are necessary to bolster the financial world’s backbone
